With 155 files across 31 directories and no sub-package export boundaries, any file inside hench can import any other file at zero friction. TypeScript path aliases or barrel-per-subdomain exports would make internal coupling visible.

hench has no internal module boundary enforcement: 155 files across 31 directories share a flat import namespace with no sub-domain barrels or path restrictions. Cross-subdomain imports (e.g. agent/tools importing store internals directly) cannot be detected by any current lint or test rule. Without explicit internal boundaries, the zone risks accumulating the same coupling entropy that produced the web-build-infrastructure artifact.

The agent/analysis sub-directory (adaptive.ts, review.ts, spin.ts, index.ts) uses a barrel-per-subdomain pattern — the cleanest internal boundary in the package — but the remaining sub-directories (agent/tools, store, prd) do not follow the same barrel convention, creating inconsistent internal navigation ergonomics

The rex-gateway.ts boundary file will grow linearly as new rex APIs are consumed by hench — there is no upper-bound policy on what is allowed into the gateway, meaning it could silently become a leaky abstraction that re-exports nearly the entire rex public surface

Extend the barrel-per-subdomain pattern (already used in agent/analysis/) to agent/tools, store, and prd sub-directories. Consistent barrel files would make the internal boundary surface explicit and prevent cross-subdomain imports from accumulating silently.

Define a maximum-scope policy for rex-gateway.ts: document which categories of rex API are in-scope for re-export (task selection, store read/write) and which are explicitly out-of-scope. Without this, the gateway will grow to mirror the entire rex public API as new hench features are added, defeating its purpose as a narrow boundary.

31 files at the root is on the larger side; confirm that documentation files (CODEX.md, PACKAGE_GUIDELINES.md, CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md) are intentionally co-located here and not better served under docs/.

The spawn-only pattern in cli.js/web.js acts as a structural firewall: orchestration scripts never import domain packages at runtime, so the monorepo-root zone cannot accidentally acquire transitive dependencies from any lower tier. This is the strongest possible isolation mechanism short of a process boundary.

Zero coupling is actively enforced by the spawn-only architecture of cli.js and web.js — any future change that introduces a direct `import` from a domain package into these scripts would immediately break the isolation guarantee and should be treated as a critical regression.

CODEX.md, PACKAGE_GUIDELINES.md, CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md, and ENFORCEMENT.md are co-located with executable orchestration scripts — these are reference documents, not runnable code, and their placement at the root adds discovery friction for new contributors who expect executable entry points at root level

Reference documents (CODEX.md, PACKAGE_GUIDELINES.md, CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md, ENFORCEMENT.md) are co-located with executable orchestration scripts (cli.js, web.js, ci.js) at the repo root. The dual-alias pairs (n-dx/ndx, sourcevision/sv) are undocumented at the alias declaration sites. Add a single canonical comment at each alias binding point stating which name is primary and which is the shorthand, so future maintainers do not accidentally deprecate the wrong one.

The two pure-test zones (monorepo-integration-tests, monorepo-e2e-tests) together cover the orchestration and cross-package contract layers that individual package test suites cannot reach — they form a complementary outer test ring and should be run in CI before package-level tests to catch integration failures early

web-dashboard at 383 files dwarfs all other zones in this batch; the rest of the codebase is well decomposed into focused zones, suggesting the web package itself may benefit from internal zone decomposition (server vs. viewer) as it continues to grow

The task-usage-analytics zone is the only non-test, non-infra zone in this batch that mixes production and test files — this is appropriate at its current size, but if the analytics surface expands it should graduate to a dedicated test sub-directory mirroring the pattern used by rex and sourcevision packages

Four of five zones have healthy or expected metrics; the sole outlier (web-build-infrastructure) is a community-detection residual caused by cross-package file mixing rather than a genuine architectural problem.

The web-viewer → usage bidirectional import cycle (3 imports one way, 1 the other) is the only cross-zone coupling in this batch worth monitoring — confirm neither direction introduces a runtime circular dependency.

The hench-agent zone's clean isolation (cohesion 1.0, coupling 0.0) despite being the largest package (155 files) is a strong indicator that the rex-gateway.ts boundary pattern is working as intended.
All four zones in this batch have cohesion at or above 0.87, indicating that the web package and monorepo test layer are well-structured with minimal internal fragmentation.

The monorepo root test zones (integration, e2e) have zero coupling to any production zone, which is the correct architecture for black-box contract testing — preserve this by never importing internal package helpers into these test files.

Cross-zone coupling in this batch is limited to the small web-dashboard ↔ task-usage-analytics bidirectional link; the overall coupling profile of these four zones is excellent and should be maintained as a benchmark.

The only runtime cross-zone coupling in this batch flows through the web-build-infrastructure residual zone, which is a tooling artifact rather than an architectural reality. Once dissolved, the six remaining zones in this batch will form two fully isolated clusters: data/doc zones (rex-runtime-state, project-documentation) with zero coupling, and executable zones (monorepo-root, hench-agent) with zero external coupling. This is an unusually clean topology for a codebase of this size.

The spawn-only pattern at the monorepo-root tier and the single-gateway pattern at the hench→rex boundary represent two complementary isolation strategies operating at different abstraction levels: process isolation (orchestration→domain) and module isolation (consumer→domain). Together they create defense-in-depth against cross-tier coupling leakage.

The project-documentation and rex-runtime-state zones share a structural property — zero import coupling — but for opposite reasons: documentation has no code relationships by nature, while rex-runtime-state is deliberately excluded from the import graph by convention. Distinguishing these two cases matters: the former is inherently safe, the latter requires active enforcement to stay safe.

The production zones (web-dashboard, task-usage-analytics) and test zones (monorepo-integration-tests, monorepo-e2e-tests) form two completely disjoint graphs with zero cross-layer coupling — this is the correct architecture for a maintainable test pyramid

The only inter-zone coupling in this entire batch is the web-dashboard ↔ task-usage-analytics bidirectional link; every other zone pair is perfectly isolated, making the 3+1 import surface the sole risk point to monitor as the codebase grows

The monorepo test zones collectively form a complementary outer ring: e2e validates user-facing CLI behavior, integration validates cross-package contracts — together they cover what individual package test suites structurally cannot reach, but the scheduler lifecycle gap in integration tests is the one missing seam

Four of the six zones in this batch achieve zero external coupling through different mechanisms (spawn isolation, gateway modules, data-only convention, or documentation-only content). The one unhealthy zone (web-build-infrastructure) is a community-detection artifact, not a genuine architectural problem. Resolving it would bring the entire batch to a uniformly clean coupling profile.

Zero coupling exists between the two test zones (monorepo-integration-tests, monorepo-e2e-tests) despite their complementary roles — this clean stratification should be treated as an architectural invariant and documented explicitly in CLAUDE.md alongside the four-tier hierarchy

The bidirectional web-viewer ↔ usage coupling (3+1 imports) is the only live cross-zone runtime coupling in the entire batch. Its small scale and documented intentionality make it low risk today, but it is the single choke point to monitor: if the usage zone gains additional consumers outside the web package, the analytics types should be promoted to a shared foundation module rather than remaining web-internal.

The sole bidirectional coupling in this batch (web-dashboard ↔ task-usage-analytics, 3+1 imports) is the only cross-zone dependency seam in all five zones; making this interface explicit via a shared type contract would reduce the risk of accidental API drift as both zones evolve

usage → web-viewer (1 import) is an inverted dependency: an analytics utility zone importing from a UI rendering zone violates the principle that lower-level modules should not depend on higher-level ones. If the usage zone needs something from web-viewer, that shared element should be extracted to a neutral location (e.g. a shared types module or foundation layer) rather than creating an upward dependency.

The web-viewer zone at 383 files has no documented internal zone decomposition. Unlike hench (which has explicit subdirectories for agent, store, prd, tools) the web-viewer appears to be a single flat zone. At this size, the absence of enforced internal boundaries means the zone cannot be incrementally extracted or tested in isolation — it will grow as a monolith until a forced decomposition becomes necessary.

The integration zone (2 files) and e2e zone (21 files) are structurally disjoint with zero coupling, but their size asymmetry (10:1 ratio) suggests integration test coverage is severely underrepresented relative to e2e. Integration tests are faster and more targeted — expanding the integration zone would improve CI feedback loop speed without sacrificing coverage depth.

The usage→web-viewer import (1 crossing, against the expected data-consumer direction) has been flagged as anomalous in previous passes but never resolved to a concrete severity. If this import is a runtime dependency of analytics on a UI component, it is a layer inversion that violates the four-tier hierarchy — the analytics layer should never depend on the presentation layer. This needs to be confirmed as either a type-only import (safe) or a runtime import (critical violation).

The monorepo has two complementary isolation strategies — process isolation (orchestration tier spawns CLIs) and module isolation (gateway pattern) — but no documented policy for which new cross-package relationships belong in each category. As the codebase grows, ad-hoc decisions about whether to cross a boundary via spawn or via gateway will accumulate without a clear decision rule.

The zero-coupling invariant of the two monorepo test zones (integration, e2e) and the single-gateway invariant of the hench and web consumer zones are both enforced today through convention and a single test file respectively. Neither invariant has a lint-level enforcement mechanism, meaning both can be silently violated during routine feature development.

The usage→web-dashboard import crosses from an analytics zone into a UI zone against the expected dependency direction. If this is a runtime import (not type-only), it constitutes a layer inversion where infrastructure depends on presentation — the inverse of the correct relationship. The import should be confirmed type-only or eliminated by extracting the shared symbol into a neutral module.

No lint-level or static-analysis enforcement exists for the gateway module purity rule (gateways re-export only, no logic). The rule is documented in CLAUDE.md and tested in domain-isolation.test.js, but a refactor that adds logic to a gateway file will not be caught until the test runs. An eslint rule restricting gateway files to re-export-only statements would provide earlier feedback.

The codebase has no documented on-boarding contract for adding a new package to the monorepo — specifically, no policy states whether a new consumer package should cross tier boundaries via spawn (orchestration pattern) or via a gateway module (hench/web pattern). This decision is currently implicit and will be made ad-hoc by the first contributor who adds a new package.

The web-viewer zone (383 files, cohesion 0.98) and hench-agent zone (155 files, cohesion 1.0) have inverse size-to-structure ratios: hench has explicit sub-directories (agent/analysis, agent/tools, store, prd) that create navigable internal structure, while web-viewer has no documented internal decomposition despite being 2.5x larger. This inconsistency in how large zones are governed across packages represents an implicit double standard.

The four-tier dependency hierarchy is documented for production code only — there is no documented tier placement for test utilities, fixtures, and factory helpers. Files in tests/fixtures/ or shared test helpers that are imported by multiple test files occupy an ambiguous position: they are neither production code nor ephemeral test assertions, and their tier membership (and therefore import rules) is undefined.

The spawn-only pattern in cli.js acts as a natural CLI-version boundary: any breaking change to a sub-CLI's command-line interface will surface as a runtime error at the spawn call site. However, there is no versioning contract or compatibility matrix for the CLI binary interfaces themselves — a semver bump in a sub-package does not guarantee the spawned command syntax is stable, and there is no integration test that validates the spawn call sites against the actual CLI argument parsers.

Two zero-coupling mechanisms coexist in the codebase — inherent zero-coupling (documentation and data zones have no code relationships by nature) and enforced zero-coupling (orchestration and test zones are kept isolated by architectural rules). The coupling metric reports both as 0 with no distinction, making it impossible to tell from metrics alone whether a zero-coupling reading represents a structural property or an active enforcement that could be violated.

The codebase has two enforcement mechanisms for architectural rules (architecture-policy.test.js for import hierarchy, domain-isolation.test.js for gateway purity) but no enforcement for the spawn-only rule at the orchestration tier. These three rules form a complete architectural invariant set, but only two of three are tested — the spawn-only rule relies entirely on code review convention.

The HTTP MCP transport layer (added per CLAUDE.md) represents a new cross-package contract surface that predates the current test infrastructure: no integration tests validate MCP session lifecycle, no e2e tests exercise tool calls over HTTP, and no architecture-policy assertion confirms the transport layer does not violate the four-tier hierarchy. The new transport is documented but unguarded.

The 'web' zone (14 files, cohesion 0.27, coupling 0.73) is the sole zone in the entire codebase with both low cohesion and high coupling — the combination that indicates fragility. All other zones are either high-cohesion (healthy) or zero-coupling data/doc zones (inherently safe). This one zone violates both metrics simultaneously and is the highest-priority structural risk in the codebase regardless of its community-detection origin.

The docs zone (19 files, cohesion 0) is growing without internal organization. At 19 files it is larger than task-usage-analytics (8 files) but has no subdirectory structure. If documentation continues to grow, the absence of internal organization will make it harder to locate relevant files — consider subdirectories mirroring the analysis/architecture/process structure already implied by the .gitkeep files visible in the git status (docs/analysis/, docs/architecture/, docs/process/).

The git status shows .sourcevision/ output files as frequently modified (M status across multiple packages). If these generated files are committed on every analysis run, they will create high-churn noise in the git history and make meaningful diffs harder to review. Consider whether .sourcevision/ output should be gitignored (treating it as a build artifact) or committed with a dedicated commit convention to separate analysis updates from code changes.

Document the decision rule for crossing tier boundaries in CLAUDE.md: when should a new cross-package relationship use spawn isolation (orchestration→domain) vs a gateway module (hench→rex pattern)? The current documentation describes both mechanisms but provides no guidance on which to choose for a new use case.

Add integration tests at the monorepo root that validate spawn call sites in cli.js against the actual CLI argument parsers of each sub-package. Currently, a breaking CLI argument change in rex or sourcevision would only be caught at runtime, not in CI.

Distinguish inherent zero-coupling (data/doc zones) from enforced zero-coupling (orchestration/test zones) in zone health reporting. Labeling both identically makes it impossible to detect enforcement regressions: a zone that should be zero-coupling by rule could quietly acquire an import with no metric change.

Define a documented tier placement for test utilities and shared fixtures. Without a clear tier assignment, shared test helpers (factories, fixture builders) may be imported by production modules accidentally, or conversely, production helpers may be duplicated in test directories because their shared-test use case was not anticipated.

The spawn-only rule for orchestration-tier scripts (cli.js, web.js, ci.js, config.js exception documented) has no automated enforcement. Add a static check — either an eslint rule or an extension to architecture-policy.test.js — that fails if orchestration-tier files contain runtime imports from package internals other than the documented config.js exception.

The HTTP MCP transport endpoints (/mcp/rex, /mcp/sourcevision) introduced per CLAUDE.md are the recommended integration path for Claude Code but have zero test coverage at any level (unit, integration, or e2e). Add at minimum one integration test validating session creation and one e2e test validating tool call round-trips before treating HTTP transport as production-ready.

Generated .sourcevision/ files appear in git status as frequently modified. Establish a policy: either gitignore all .sourcevision/ output (treating it as a build artifact regenerated on demand) or adopt a commit convention (e.g. 'chore(sv): regenerate analysis') to isolate high-churn generated files from meaningful code history.

The docs/ directory has three subdirectories (analysis/, architecture/, process/) indicated by .gitkeep files but no documented convention for which content belongs in each. As documentation grows, the absence of a filing convention will cause ad-hoc placement. Add a brief docs/README.md or CLAUDE.md entry describing what belongs in each subdirectory.

concurrent-execution-metrics.ts name implies it tracks hench agent execution concurrency. Verify this file does not import from the hench package — such an import would violate the four-tier hierarchy (web is domain tier, hench is execution tier, domain must not import from execution).

pr-markdown-refresh-diagnostics.ts is a utility file with a name specific enough to suggest it should live in a dedicated PR-workflow zone or in rex rather than in the general web server layer. Review whether this file has domain logic that belongs upstream in the dependency hierarchy.

architecture-policy.test.js is the only zone in the entire codebase that enforces the four-tier dependency hierarchy as a runtime assertion — it is a single point of failure for import-boundary enforcement; consider whether additional policy checks (e.g. gateway module purity) should live here

Zero coupling is actively enforced by the spawn-only architecture of cli.js and web.js — any future change that introduces a direct `import` from a domain package into these scripts would immediately break the isolation guarantee and should be treated as a critical regression.

The data-layer contract (never imported at runtime) is convention-only. Consider adding a lint rule or CI check that asserts no `.ts`/`.js` source file contains an import path resolving into `.rex/` to make the contract machine-enforceable rather than doc-only.

The cleanup scheduler (usage-cleanup-scheduler.ts) is a stateful process-level concern but is absent from monorepo-integration-tests — if the scheduler interacts with rex or sourcevision stores at startup, that contract is currently untested at the integration boundary

The rex-gateway.ts boundary module is the sole interface between hench-agent and the rex domain — this single-gateway pattern means any future rex API change has exactly one file to update in hench, and any unintended direct import from rex would be immediately visible in a diff. This is the cleanest cross-package interface pattern in the codebase.

scheduler-startup.test.js in monorepo-integration-tests partially overlaps the concern of task-usage-analytics scheduler tests — confirm there is no test duplication and that the integration test validates cross-package startup ordering rather than internal scheduler logic

docs/architecture/ files are referenced from CLAUDE.md but have no automated freshness check. If architectural decisions change (e.g. the four-tier hierarchy evolves or gateway rules shift), these documents will silently become stale without any tooling signal.

The co-location of packages/hench/src/store/suggestions.ts with web package files in this zone creates a false import-graph edge between the hench and web tiers. Any zone-coupling metric that includes this zone will over-report cross-tier coupling until suggestions.ts is reassigned to hench-agent.

The single usage→web-dashboard import is the only dependency that flows against the expected data-consumer direction (viewer should pull from analytics, not the reverse); verify this import is not a hidden circular initialization dependency

hench has no internal module boundary enforcement: 155 files across 31 directories share a flat import namespace with no sub-domain barrels or path restrictions. Cross-subdomain imports (e.g. agent/tools importing store internals directly) cannot be detected by any current lint or test rule. Without explicit internal boundaries, the zone risks accumulating the same coupling entropy that produced the web-build-infrastructure artifact.

architecture-policy.test.js is a single point of failure for four-tier hierarchy enforcement. If this file is skipped, broken, or omitted from a CI run, cross-tier import violations can merge undetected. The policy should be backed by at least one redundant mechanism (e.g. an eslint import boundary rule or a dedicated CI step that runs before package tests).

Only 2 integration test files exist at the monorepo boundary. As cross-package interactions grow (rex↔sourcevision data flow, hench↔rex task selection, web↔rex MCP contract), the integration ring has no mechanism to enforce proportional growth — unlike the e2e suite which has an architecture-policy guard. A test coverage policy for cross-package contracts is absent.

Reference documents (CODEX.md, PACKAGE_GUIDELINES.md, CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md, ENFORCEMENT.md) are co-located with executable orchestration scripts (cli.js, web.js, ci.js) at the repo root. This conflates two distinct concerns — documentation artifacts and runnable entry points — making it harder to audit the orchestration surface at a glance. Move reference docs to docs/ to make the root a clean entry-point manifest.

The two-phase pending-proposals.json → acknowledged-findings.json workflow has no crash-recovery sentinel. A tool run that terminates between the two writes leaves both files in an inconsistent state with no machine-detectable signal. This is a data-integrity gap that could cause silent PRD corruption on restart.

shared-types.ts defines types consumed cross-zone by web-dashboard but lives inside the analytics zone with no explicit export contract. Consumers reach into zone-internal types rather than through a stable interface boundary, making the contract implicit and fragile if the zone is ever refactored.

The phantom zone's 0.73 coupling score is being counted in aggregate web-package health metrics. Any CI gate or automated report that sums or averages zone coupling will treat the web package as unhealthy due solely to a community-detection artifact, not a real architectural problem. This false signal may cause teams to ignore legitimate future coupling regressions because the baseline is already flagged.

Zone mixes Node.js server code and browser Preact viewer code in a single zone. These two sub-environments are mutually exclusive at runtime — Node modules (http, fs) cannot load in the browser and vice versa. A split into web-server and web-viewer sub-zones would enforce the runtime boundary structurally, not just by convention.
